content:
Mutual information grew out of Claude Shannon's 1948 formulation of information theory, where entropy H(X) quantified uncertainty in a single source. Researchers soon needed a symmetric measure of **shared** information between two sources—how much knowing one channel output reduces uncertainty about another.

The modern definition **I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y)** appears in Shannon's framework and was developed further by information theorists studying noisy communication channels. In a channel, I(input; output) measures how much of the input survives the noise—directly linked to channel capacity.

In machine learning, the same quantity resurfaced under different names. Decision-tree **information gain** is mutual information between a feature split and the class label. Feature-selection literature in the 1990s–2000s popularized ranking inputs by I(X_i; Y). Clustering evaluation adopted **normalized mutual information** to compare partitions without requiring matching cluster IDs.

Today I(X; Y) sits at the center of information-theoretic ML: feature filters, information bottleneck methods, and representation-learning bounds all express their objectives in terms of mutual information or tractable surrogates.

timeline.label: 1948 — Shannon's information theory
timeline.description: Entropy and conditional entropy provide the language; mutual information emerges as the reduction in uncertainty when one variable is observed.

timeline.label: 1960s–1970s — Multi-user and channel capacity
timeline.description: Information theorists formalize I(X; Y) in joint source-channel problems; MI becomes the standard dependence measure in communications.

timeline.label: 1980s–1990s — ML feature selection
timeline.description: Filter methods rank features by information gain / mutual information with targets, especially for discrete classification.

timeline.label: 2000s–present — NMI and deep learning
timeline.description: Normalized MI benchmarks clustering; information bottleneck and variational MI bounds connect I(X; Y) to representation learning and Gen AI training objectives.
